name: LSTM
summary: Long Short-Term Memory is a gated recurrent neural network architecture that uses input, forget, and output gates to control the flow of information through cell states, effectively solving the vanishing gradient problem that plagued vanilla RNNs. It became the dominant approach for sequential modeling in NLP, speech, and time-series before the rise of Transformer architectures.
use_when: Sequential data with moderate length where temporal order is critical and interpretability of hidden state dynamics matters. Small to medium datasets where Transformer-level compute is unnecessary. Edge deployment scenarios with strict memory constraints.
avoid_when: Very long sequences where attention mechanisms capture dependencies more effectively. Tasks requiring parallel training across large clusters where RNN sequentiality becomes a bottleneck. State-of-the-art NLP tasks where Transformers have established dominance.
pros: Explicitly preserves sequential order through recurrent state propagation, making it inherently position-aware without external positional encoding
Lower memory footprint than Transformers during inference because it does not store per-token attention matrices or key-value caches
Interpretable gate activations (input, forget, output) reveal which information is retained or discarded at each time step
No positional encoding required, avoiding the aliasing and length extrapolation issues seen in learned sinusoidal position embeddings
cons: Cannot parallelize training across the time dimension, forcing sequential backpropagation through time that scales linearly with sequence length
Performance degrades on very long sequences due to compounding gradient noise and limited capacity for long-range state retention
Slower training than Transformers on modern hardware because matrix multiplications are not batched across the sequence axis
Largely replaced by Transformers and Mamba-style architectures in production NLP systems, resulting in limited pretrained model availability

key_hyperparams.name: input_size
key_hyperparams.note: Number of expected features in the input x. Must match the embedding dimension of your input data. No default; must be specified explicitly.

key_hyperparams.name: hidden_size
key_hyperparams.note: Number of features in the hidden state h. Defines the model capacity. Common values range from 128 for small tasks to 512 for language modeling.

key_hyperparams.name: num_layers
key_hyperparams.note: Number of stacked recurrent layers. Increase to 2-4 for deeper feature extraction but watch for vanishing gradients across stacked layers.

key_hyperparams.name: dropout
key_hyperparams.note: Dropout probability applied to outputs of each LSTM layer except the last. Set to 0.2-0.5 during training to prevent overfitting on small datasets.

key_hyperparams.name: bidirectional
key_hyperparams.note: Whether to use bidirectional LSTM processing both forward and backward contexts. Set to 'true' for classification tasks requiring full context awareness.

quick_start:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

lstm = nn.LSTM(
    input_size=128,
    hidden_size=256,
    num_layers=2,
    dropout=0.3,
    bidirectional=True,
    batch_first=True
)

x = torch.randn(32, 50, 128)  # batch, seq, features
output, (hidden, cell) = lstm(x)
decision_notes: Choose LSTM for moderate-length sequences where interpretability and memory efficiency matter. Use Transformer for long-context or parallel training requirements.
